svg.text: Full Bohr-model diagrams for C, Fe, Se, Nd — colored dots = electrons by subshell · empty circles = unfilled slots
Each column = one element. Colored squares = electrons added beyond the previous noble gas core. Rows = subshells s/p/d/f. Brightness = filled vs empty slot.
Understanding Electron Shells, Subshells & the Aufbau Principle
Shells (n=1,2,3…): The main energy levels around the nucleus. Each shell holds at most 2n² electrons.
Subshells (s,p,d,f): Each shell is divided into subshells of different shapes. Max electrons: s=2, p=6, d=10, f=14.
The Bohr diagrams above show each subshell as an arc on its shell ring. Colored dots = filled electrons, empty circles = vacant slots.
Electrons fill the lowest-energy subshell first. The order is NOT simply shell by shell — it follows energy level crossings:
Notice: 4s fills BEFORE 3d (lower energy), and 6s fills before both 4f and 5d. This explains why the periodic table has its distinctive shape.
Why the table is shaped the way it is:
s-block (2 cols): only one s-subshell per shell, max 2 electrons.
p-block (6 cols): only one p-subshell per shell, max 6 electrons.
d-block (10 cols): only one d-subshell per shell, max 10 electrons.
f-block (14 cols): only one f-subshell per shell, max 14 electrons.
Each element column shows only the electrons added on top of the previous noble gas core.
The filled-bar length is proportional to how many electrons occupy that subshell vs its maximum.
A full s bar + partial d bar = transition metal. Full s + growing f = lanthanide/actinide. Etc.
The full electron configs (abbreviated) are shown in each cell of the enriched 18-column dark table (periodic_table_v3_dark.svg).
The 32-column table (periodic_table_32col.svg) shows the blocks in their natural order: s | f | d | p — directly reflecting the Aufbau filling sequence.
